title: Medical Lab Scientist

rationales.taskAutomation: Standard assay result flagging and QC documentation are automatable; complex specimen troubleshooting, method validation and abnormal-result interpretation are not.
rationales.toolMaturity: Lab automation and AI-based result-flagging systems are credible and improving, increasingly standard in larger reference and hospital labs.
rationales.adoption: Automated analyzers and AI flagging are common in large reference labs and hospital systems, spreading into smaller labs.
rationales.agenticExposure: Agents can own routine result flagging and QC documentation, but complex troubleshooting and method development stay with the scientist.

tasksGrowing: Complex specimen troubleshooting and abnormal-result interpretation
Lab automation system oversight and quality management
salarySource: BLS OES May 2024, SOC 29-2011 (Medical and Clinical Laboratory Technologists), p25-p75 rounded
insight: AI-based result flagging now catches most routine abnormalities automatically, but validating new assays and troubleshooting complex specimens still requires a trained scientist's judgment. Labs are shifting scientists toward automation oversight rather than routine testing.
